title: Run your first program
instruction:
Every Rust program starts at `fn main()`. The code inside the curly braces runs when you execute the program.

`println!` is a macro that prints a line. The `!` tells you it is a macro, not a regular function.

Press **Run** and see what happens.
successMessage: You just ran a Rust program. That fn main() is the entry point — every Rust binary you ever write starts there.

title: Print something different
instruction:
Change the text inside the quotes to your name and run it.

The string is between the double quotes. Everything else must stay exactly as it is.
hint: Only change the text between the quotes. Keep fn main(), the curly braces, and println! exactly as they are.

codeChecks.message: Change the text inside the quotes to something personal.

successMessage: You told the computer to say something specific. That is programming — giving precise instructions.

title: Print two lines
instruction:
Each `println!` prints one line.

Add a second `println!` below the first one and run it. Print two different things.
hint: Add println!("your text"); on a new line inside the curly braces.

codeChecks.message: Add a second println! call below the first.

successMessage: Rust ran both lines, top to bottom. That sequential execution is the foundation of every program.

title: Print a number
instruction:
`println!` can print numbers too, using `{}` as a placeholder:

```rust
println!("{}", 42);
```

The `{}` gets replaced by the value after the comma.

Run this and see the output.
successMessage: The {} placeholder works with numbers, decimals, and booleans. It is Rust's way of injecting values into strings.

title: Multiple placeholders
instruction:
You can use several `{}` in one `println!`:

```rust
println!("{} + {} = {}", 1, 2, 3);
```

Each `{}` maps to the next argument in order.

Run this program.
successMessage: Each {} slot is filled left to right. If you have three {}, you need exactly three values after the format string.

title: Comments in Rust
instruction:
Comments are notes for humans. Rust ignores them completely.

`//` starts a single-line comment. Everything after `//` on that line is ignored.

Run this — the comments do not appear in output.
successMessage: Comments document WHY your code does something. Write code that explains itself, and comments that explain the reasoning behind decisions.

title: Fix the syntax error
instruction:
This program has a bug. Run it, read the error message, and fix it.

Rust's error messages are famous for being helpful — they tell you exactly what is wrong.
hint: Look at the println! line. Check the quotes and the semicolon carefully.
successMessage: Rust's compiler is your friend. It catches mistakes before your program runs — unlike many other languages. Get comfortable reading its error messages.

title: Write from scratch
instruction:
The function body is empty. Write a complete Rust program that prints three lines:
- `Rust is fast`
- `Rust is safe`
- `Rust is fun`
hint: Add three println! calls inside fn main().
successMessage: You wrote a working Rust program from scratch. fn main is the container, println! is the tool. That is the whole skeleton.
